title: Sports Psychology: Differential Recognition, RED-S, and the Referral Network
summary: Go beyond recognize-support-connect: tell normal post-injury adjustment from a clinical concern, understand the biopsychosocial model of recovery, recognize RED-S at real medical depth, and know the limits of confidentiality when safety is on the line.

sections.heading: Normal adjustment or a clinical concern? A differential lens
body: You already know that frustration, low mood, and irritability after a significant injury are normal — a grief-like response to a real loss, not an overreaction. The deeper question this unit adds: how do you tell that expected adjustment apart from something that has crossed into needing a professional evaluation? Three things matter — not any one of them alone, but the pattern together.

Duration relative to recovery trajectory: normal adjustment tends to track with healing — an athlete feels worse right after the injury and gradually better as rehab milestones pass, even with rough days along the way. A mood that isn't improving as recovery progresses, or that's getting worse instead of better, is a signal something beyond the injury itself may be going on.

Severity and functional impairment: normal adjustment doesn't usually wreck basic functioning. If sleep, appetite, schoolwork, or the ability to show up and engage with life falls apart — not just "having a hard week," but a real breakdown in day-to-day function — that's a different category of finding than expected grief.
list: Normal adjustment: proportional to the loss, fluctuates day to day, gradually improves alongside recovery, basic functioning stays mostly intact.

Signal for professional evaluation: duration mismatched with recovery progress, severity out of proportion to the loss, or real functional impairment (can't sleep, can't eat, can't engage with school or people).

callout.title: This is a differential lens, not a diagnostic one
callout.text: You are not being asked to decide whether it's "adjustment disorder" versus "depression" — that's a licensed clinician's call. This differential lens exists so you recognize when something has moved from "expected and will pass" to "needs a professional now," and route it there faster.

sections.heading: The biopsychosocial model of injury recovery
body: Rehab plans are often written as if recovery is purely physical: heal the tissue, restore strength and range of motion, clear the athlete. The biopsychosocial model is the reminder that three interacting factors drive recovery, not one — physical (tissue healing, pain, strength), psychological (mood, motivation, fear of re-injury, self-efficacy), and social/team context (coach communication, teammate connection, playing-time pressure, family expectations).

These factors aren't independent — they feed each other. An athlete who feels forgotten by the team during a long recovery (social) is more likely to lose motivation for home exercises (psychological), which shows up as inconsistent rehab attendance the physical therapist reads as a compliance problem, when the actual gap started somewhere else entirely.

This is why "just do your PT" is an incomplete plan for an athlete who is struggling. The exercises can be perfectly prescribed and the athlete can still stall out if the psychological or social piece is unaddressed — which is exactly why a full sports medicine team includes more than a physical therapist.

sections.heading: What a sport psychology consultant actually does
body: Sport psychology consultants work with athletes on performance-related mental skills — a distinct professional role from a clinical psychologist or psychiatrist who diagnoses and treats mental health disorders, though the two sometimes work together on the same athlete's care team.

During rehab specifically, that work often includes imagery (mentally rehearsing the movement in vivid, sensory detail to help maintain the neural patterns and confidence a skill relies on, even while the athlete physically can't perform it), goal-setting (breaking a long recovery into concrete process goals — like today's range-of-motion target — so an athlete has something to work toward instead of just "waiting to get better"), and confidence or self-talk work aimed at rebuilding trust in the injured body part.

None of that is something a student athletic-training aide performs. It's included here so you understand what a referral to "the sport psych" actually means and can describe it accurately to a curious or hesitant athlete — your role stays recognize, support, and connect, exactly as in the earlier unit.

sections.heading: RED-S: the medical urgency behind the pattern
body: You've already seen Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S) covered as a nutrition and overuse-injury topic — chronic low energy availability disrupting bone, hormonal, metabolic, and immune function. The psychology-adjacent piece is why it belongs here too: RED-S is frequently intertwined with disordered eating, body image pressure, and a psychological need for control, and recognizing it requires the same seriousness as any other medical red flag, not a discipline conversation.

Two signs deserve real weight. Missed or irregular menstrual cycles are a medical sign, not a lifestyle detail or a badge of hard training — the body pausing reproductive function is a direct signal that available energy has dropped too low to support normal physiology. Recurrent bone stress injuries — a second or third stress fracture without an obvious, isolated high-impact mechanism — are the same kind of red flag from the overuse-injury side: bone that keeps failing under normal training load is often bone that isn't getting the energy or hormonal support it needs to remodel properly.

Neither sign, alone or together, is something a student aide investigates or manages. The job is recognizing the pattern and referring it promptly and privately to the athletic trainer or team physician, who can loop in a dietitian or physician as needed.

callout.title: RED-S is medical, never a discipline issue
callout.text: Never confront an athlete yourself about eating, weight, or body composition, and never frame missed cycles or recurrent stress fractures as a willpower or commitment problem. This is a medical issue that needs a sports medicine and nutrition team — sensitive, prompt referral is the entire job.

sections.heading: Transition and identity: when the injury ends the season, or the career
body: The 9-10 unit covered season-ending injury as a bigger loss than a routine one. Here's the deeper mechanism: when athletic identity — how strongly a person's sense of self is built around being an athlete — is very tightly held, a career-ending injury doesn't just end a season. It can trigger a genuine identity crisis: loss of daily structure, loss of a social group built around the team, and loss of a future the athlete had already planned their identity around.

This is a predictable part of many athletic careers ending — whether by injury, being cut, or simply aging out of a sport — not a rare or unusual reaction. Recognizing it as predictable is part of taking it seriously: an athlete in this position benefits from connection to a counselor or sport psychology professional as a normal, proactive step, the same way you'd route a suspected fracture to a physician rather than waiting to see if it's "really that bad."

sections.heading: Destigmatizing with real numbers
body: It's easy to talk about athlete mental health in the abstract. The factual context matters more: national surveys of college athletes have repeatedly found that a substantial share — commonly cited figures put it around a third — report experiencing clinically meaningful symptoms of anxiety or depression at some point during their athletic career. That's not a rare edge case; it's common enough that most teams will have more than one athlete dealing with it in a given season, whether or not it's visible.

That context is why the recognize-support-connect framework isn't overkill for a rare problem — it's a routine skill for a common one, the same way knowing how to tape an ankle is routine because ankle sprains are common.

sections.heading: The expanded aide role: patterns, documentation, and the limits of confidentiality
body: At this level, your role expands past noticing one bad day. Watch for patterns across a season — an athlete who trends worse before every big competition, or who was fine early in the year but has been declining steadily since a return from injury. A single data point is easy to explain away; a pattern across weeks is much harder to dismiss and much more useful information for the athletic trainer or counselor who takes it from there.

Brief, factual documentation supports that: a dated note like "missed team meeting again, seemed withdrawn at practice" is more useful to the professional who follows up than a vague memory weeks later. You're also a bridge to resources — knowing what your team or school actually offers (athletic trainer, school counselor, a campus or community mental health line) so you can make a specific connection instead of a vague "you should talk to someone."

Confidentiality has real limits here. In general, you can keep a teammate's disclosure private and simply offer support without broadcasting it to the team — that's appropriate and often exactly what they need. But safety concerns always override confidentiality: any mention of self-harm, suicide, or abuse gets escalated to a trusted adult immediately, no exceptions, regardless of what you were asked to keep quiet.

callout.title: Privacy stops where safety starts
callout.text: You can honor a request for privacy about everyday struggles. You cannot honor a request for secrecy about self-harm, suicidal talk, or abuse — those always get reported to a trusted adult right away. This isn't a betrayal of trust; it's the entire reason the limit exists.

sections.heading: Crisis recognition, reinforced: the fuller warning-sign picture
body: The 9-10 protocol still applies exactly as taught: any mention of suicide or self-harm — direct or indirect, serious-sounding or not — gets taken seriously every time, with no promise of secrecy, staying with the person if it's safe to do so, and getting an adult or calling 911 immediately. This unit adds a fuller picture of the warning signs that should trigger that same response, because a crisis doesn't always announce itself with a direct statement.

This is still recognition, not assessment — you are never trained or expected to judge how serious a risk is. Any one of these signs is reason enough to escalate; you don't need certainty, and you're never wrong to act on it.
list: Withdrawal — pulling away from close relationships and routines all at once, more abrupt than the gradual quietness covered in the earlier unit.
Giving away meaningful possessions — items with sentimental or practical value handed off with no clear, ordinary reason.
A sudden sense of calm or relief after a visible period of crisis or distress — this can mean a decision has been made, not that things have gotten better.

Direct statements ("I want to die," "I'm going to kill myself") and indirect statements ("everyone would be better off without me," "I won't be around much longer," "it doesn't matter anymore") — treat both categories with equal seriousness.

callout.title: Any one of these signs — act, don't wait for confirmation
callout.text: Withdrawal, giving away possessions, sudden calm after crisis, or any direct or indirect statement about not wanting to be alive: treat each as sufficient on its own. Don't promise secrecy, stay with them if it's safe, and get a trusted adult involved immediately — or call 911 for immediate danger.

quiz.question: An athlete three weeks post-ACL surgery still seems quieter and sadder than usual around teammates, but she's sleeping fine, eating normally, and her mood visibly lifts each time she's cleared for a new rehab phase. How should this be understood?
choices: A normal grief-like adjustment that's tracking with her recovery progress — not a red flag on its own
A clear sign of major depression requiring immediate psychiatric referral
Proof she isn't mentally tough enough for the sport
Something to ignore completely, since mood after injury never matters
quiz.explanation: Mood improving alongside recovery milestones, with no disruption to sleep, eating, or engagement, fits the pattern of normal adjustment covered in this unit's differential lens. A referral flag would come from duration mismatched with recovery, out-of-proportion severity, or real functional impairment — none of which is present here.

quiz.question: A rehabbing athlete has excellent physical therapy attendance and objectively good tissue healing, but has stopped coming to team meetings, feels forgotten by teammates, and has quit doing the mental rehearsal work assigned by the sport psychology consultant. Which concept best explains why 'just do your PT' isn't a complete plan here?
choices: Tissue healing time always determines return-to-play regardless of anything else
Athletes who skip mental rehearsal simply aren't committed to their sport
Team meetings have no real bearing on an individual athlete's physical rehab outcome
The biopsychosocial model — physical, psychological, and social/team factors interact, so a gap in the social piece can stall recovery even when tissue healing looks fine
quiz.explanation: The biopsychosocial model explains exactly this: feeling disconnected from the team (social) can erode motivation and follow-through on rehab work (psychological), even while the tissue itself (physical) is healing on schedule. Physical progress alone doesn't guarantee a smooth recovery.

quiz.question: Which of the following is something a licensed sport psychology consultant does with an injured athlete — and NOT something a student athletic-training aide should attempt?
choices: Guiding structured imagery/visualization work and rehab goal-setting to support motivation and confidence
Listening without judgment and connecting the athlete to a trusted adult
Noticing a concerning pattern and reporting it to the athletic trainer
Keeping brief, factual documentation of a teammate seeming off over several weeks
quiz.explanation: Imagery, structured goal-setting, and confidence work are performance-mental-skills techniques delivered by a credentialed sport psychology consultant. The other three options are exactly the recognize-support-connect and documentation role a student aide is expected to perform.

quiz.question: A distance runner has had two bone stress injuries this year, hasn't had a menstrual cycle in four months, and says she's 'just really dedicated to training.' What is the correct read on this situation?
choices: She's simply a hard worker showing strong commitment — no action needed
This is a likely RED-S pattern — recurrent bone stress injury plus missed cycles is a medical red flag requiring prompt referral to the athletic trainer or physician, not a discipline conversation
Confront her directly about her eating habits so she changes them
Wait to see if a third bone stress injury happens before doing anything
quiz.explanation: Recurrent bone stress injury combined with missed menstrual cycles is exactly the RED-S recognition pattern covered in this unit — both are medical signs of low energy availability, not evidence of dedication, and they call for prompt, private referral, never a peer confrontation or a wait-and-see approach.

quiz.question: A senior's career-ending shoulder injury means she'll never play again. She tells you sport was 'basically her whole identity' and she doesn't know who she is without it. What's the most appropriate response?
choices: Tell her to just focus on academics now that sport is over
Connect her with a counselor or sport psychology professional, since this identity-crisis reaction is a predictable, serious part of career-ending transitions, not something to power through alone
Avoid bringing it up so you don't make her feel worse
Reassure her that this feeling will resolve on its own without any outside help
quiz.explanation: A career-ending injury for an athlete with strongly held athletic identity is a predictable, serious transition, and professional support is a normal, proactive step — the same logic as referring a suspected fracture rather than waiting to see how bad it gets. Dismissing it, avoiding it, or assuming it resolves alone all miss that this is common and treatable.

quiz.question: What does research on college athlete mental health generally show about the prevalence of meaningful anxiety or depression symptoms?
choices: It's extremely rare, affecting only a tiny fraction of athletes
It only occurs in individual sports, never team sports
It's meaningfully common — national surveys have found a substantial share, commonly cited around a third, of college athletes report clinically meaningful symptoms at some point
It only affects athletes who have already been injured
quiz.explanation: Survey data consistently shows this is common, not rare — roughly a third of college athletes in commonly cited figures, across sport types and regardless of injury history. That prevalence is exactly why recognize-support-connect is treated as a routine skill, not a rare-emergency one.

quiz.question: A teammate tells you she's been struggling with anxiety and asks you to keep it between the two of you. Later in the same conversation she mentions she's been cutting herself. What's the right move?
choices: Honor her original request for privacy completely, since she asked first
Keep the anxiety part private and continue supporting her quietly, but the self-harm disclosure overrides confidentiality — escalate that to a trusted adult right away
Wait until she brings up the self-harm again before doing anything
Tell the whole team so everyone can rally around her
quiz.explanation: Everyday struggles can generally stay private while you offer quiet support, but a safety concern like self-harm always overrides confidentiality and must be escalated immediately, regardless of what was said earlier in the conversation. Waiting or broadcasting to the whole team are both wrong responses to a safety disclosure.

quiz.question: A teammate who has seemed distressed for weeks suddenly appears unusually calm and starts giving away a few of his prized possessions to friends. What should this pattern raise concern for, and what should you do?
choices: He's simply feeling better — no action needed
He's decluttering before a move — ask him casually sometime this week
A possible warning sign that a crisis decision has been made — treat it seriously and get a trusted adult involved immediately
He's showing maturity by being generous — compliment him on it
quiz.explanation: Sudden calm after a period of visible crisis and giving away meaningful possessions are both warning signs covered in this unit — either one alone is reason enough to escalate immediately to a trusted adult, without waiting for a direct statement or more certainty.

flashcards.front: Differential signal: normal adjustment vs. needs professional evaluation
flashcards.back: Normal adjustment tracks with recovery and leaves basic functioning intact. A referral flag: duration mismatched with recovery progress, severity out of proportion, or real functional impairment (can't sleep, eat, or engage).

Physical (tissue healing), psychological (mood, fear of re-injury, motivation), and social/team context (coach, teammates, pressure) all interact — a gap in one can stall recovery even when the others look fine.

flashcards.front: What a sport psychology consultant does
flashcards.back: Performance-focused mental skills work: imagery/visualization, rehab goal-setting, and confidence/self-talk work. A credentialed professional's role — not something a student aide performs.
A medical sign of significant low energy availability, treated like an abnormal vital sign — never a badge of hard training or a lifestyle detail.
A second or third stress fracture without an obvious high-impact mechanism is a red flag for underlying low energy availability, linking directly back to overuse-injury risk.

flashcards.front: RED-S response — never a discipline issue
flashcards.back: Suspected RED-S or disordered eating always routes to the athletic trainer/physician/dietitian team — never a peer confrontation about eating, weight, or willpower.

When identity is tightly built around being an athlete, a career-ending injury can trigger a genuine identity crisis, not just grief. A predictable part of many career endings — referral to a counselor or sport psych professional is a normal, proactive step.
National surveys of college athletes commonly find around a third report clinically meaningful anxiety or depression symptoms at some point — common, not rare.
Watch for trends across weeks or a season (not just one bad day), and keep brief, factual, dated documentation to give the AT or counselor the full picture.

General struggles can often stay private while you support quietly. Self-harm, suicidal talk, or abuse always override confidentiality and must be escalated to a trusted adult immediately, no exceptions.
Withdrawal, giving away meaningful possessions, sudden calm after a period of crisis, and direct or indirect statements about not wanting to be alive — any one is reason enough to escalate.

flashcards.front: Recognize, support, connect — still the aide's ceiling
flashcards.back: Even with deeper knowledge of RED-S, the biopsychosocial model, and crisis signs, the student aide's role stays recognize, support, and connect — never diagnose, counsel, or deliver a professional's intervention.
